skill.name: happyflow-generator
skill.description: Generates Python happy-path API test scripts from OpenAPI specifications and GraphQL schemas, then optionally executes them with retries and reporting.
skill.summary: Generate and run happy-path API tests from OpenAPI or GraphQL inputs.
skill.author: Chaim12345

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified network access, filesystem use, environment access, and subprocess execution in SKILL.md. The behavior appears aligned with an API test-generation skill, but generated scripts embed credentials and execute outbound API calls, so publication should include a clear warning.

medium_findings.title: Generated Script Executes Network Requests With User Credentials
medium_findings.description: The skill prepares bearer, API key, basic, and OAuth credentials, then generates a Python script that serializes AUTH_HEADERS and sends requests through a requests session. This is legitimate for API testing but can expose secrets if generated scripts, logs, or outputs are shared.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential handling and request generation are explicit and central to the skill. The risk is contextual because the target API and credentials are supplied by the user.

medium_findings.title: Subprocess Execution of Generated Python Script
medium_findings.description: The retry workflow writes generated Python to a temporary file and executes it with subprocess.run. This enables the intended test flow, but it also means generated code can perform network actions and should only be run in a controlled environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is direct and uses a temporary generated script. There is no shell=True evidence, so command injection is less likely than execution-risk from generated code.

medium_findings.title: Detailed Reports May Reveal API Data
medium_findings.description: The generated script stores request payloads, response headers, and the first 1000 characters of response bodies for reporting. This can expose sensitive API data in console output or saved reports.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly captures response and request details for reporting. The sensitivity depends on the tested API and returned data.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Findings Are Markdown Code Fences
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown code fences around Python examples, not Ruby command execution. No shell backtick execution evidence was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown fence delimiters or examples. The actual executable command risk is already captured by the subprocess finding.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Not Cryptographic Use
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts map to descriptive text, response descriptions, and Basic auth base64 encoding. Base64 is encoding, not encryption, and no custom cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only concrete encoding operation is standard Basic authentication header construction. No evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak cryptographic algorithms was found.

low_findings.title: Temporary File Cleanup Uses Expected Local Filesystem Operations
low_findings.description: The skill writes generated scripts to a temporary file and deletes the file afterward. This is expected for local execution, but users should avoid running it with sensitive working-directory permissions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is direct and limited to a temporary generated script. The cleanup path is explicit, but any generated script execution still carries operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Calls to User-Supplied URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: The parser fetches OpenAPI specifications from HTTP URLs, obtains OAuth tokens from a supplied token URL, and generated tests call API endpoints. This is a true positive network pattern with legitimate API-testing purpose.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The requests calls are explicit and accept user-controlled URLs. No evidence shows a hardcoded exfiltration destination.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credentials Embedded in Generated Output
dangerous_patterns.description: The generated script includes AUTH_HEADERS built from supplied authentication data. This can place tokens or API keys in returned scripts and terminal history.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script-generation code serializes headers and the example prints the final script. Exposure depends on whether real credentials are used.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Code Execution Loop
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly executes generated code and modifies it after certain HTTP errors. This is useful for test repair but should be treated as generated code execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The retry loop, subprocess execution, and script mutation are all visible in the same function. The implementation avoids shell invocation but still runs generated code.

content.value_statement: API teams need quick coverage for normal endpoint flows, but manual scripts are slow to maintain. This skill turns OpenAPI or GraphQL inputs into Python happy-path tests with ordering, retries, and reporting.
actual_capabilities: Parses OpenAPI sources from local files or HTTP URLs.
Creates a simplified GraphQL test representation.
Builds authentication headers for bearer, API key, basic, and OAuth client credentials.
Orders endpoint calls using inferred dependencies and HTTP method priority.
Generates Python requests-based scripts with retry logic and response validation.
Can execute generated scripts and retry simple fixes after common errors.
limitations: Generated tests focus on successful 2xx happy paths, not negative or abuse cases.
GraphQL handling is simplified and may need manual schema-specific edits.
Generated scripts may contain credentials if real secrets are provided.
Execution can send real requests to live APIs unless users choose a safe target.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap API Smoke Tests
use_cases.description: Generate a first working Python smoke test suite from an OpenAPI document.

use_cases.title: Validate Release Flows
use_cases.description: Run ordered happy-path requests before a release to confirm core endpoints still return success responses.

use_cases.title: Explore Partner APIs
use_cases.description: Create readable scripts that show authentication, request order, generated payloads, and response checks.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Basic Test Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a happy-path Python API test script from this OpenAPI file. Use placeholder credentials and do not execute the script.

prompt_templates.title: Add Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate happy-path tests for this API specification using bearer authentication. Keep the token as an environment variable placeholder.

prompt_templates.title: Order Dependent Endpoints
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this OpenAPI specification, infer endpoint dependencies, and generate a Python script that creates resources before reading, updating, or deleting them.

prompt_templates.title: Run and Repair in a Sandbox
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate the happy-path script, execute it only against my sandbox API, report failures, and suggest minimal safe fixes without exposing secrets.

output_examples.input: OpenAPI specification for a task service with create, list, update, and delete endpoints.
output_examples.output: A Python test flow that creates a task, stores its identifier, reads it, updates it, validates successful responses, and prints a summary.

output_examples.input: GraphQL endpoint description with a bearer token requirement.
output_examples.output: A generated Python request sequence for a basic GraphQL query using an authorization header placeholder.

output_examples.input: API specification with rate limits and response schemas.
output_examples.output: A happy-path script with retry behavior for rate limits and schema validation for successful JSON responses.

best_practices: Run generated scripts against sandbox or staging APIs before production.
Use placeholders or environment variables instead of hardcoded real credentials.
Review generated requests and payloads before enabling execution.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated tests against production APIs without rate and data controls.
Do not paste long-lived secrets into prompts or generated scripts.
Do not treat generated happy-path tests as complete security or negative testing.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: Yes. Its workflow can write and run generated Python scripts, so execution should happen in a controlled environment.

faq.question: Does it support OpenAPI and GraphQL?
faq.answer: Yes. OpenAPI parsing is the main workflow, while GraphQL support is simplified and may need manual changes.

faq.question: Can it use authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports bearer tokens, API keys, basic auth, and OAuth client credentials.

faq.question: Will it test error cases?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on happy-path calls that should return 2xx status codes.

faq.question: Can generated scripts expose secrets?
faq.answer: Yes. If real credentials are supplied, they can appear in generated scripts or logs.

faq.question: Is it safe for live production APIs?
faq.answer: Use caution. The generated script can create, update, or delete data depending on the API specification.
